turns.content: I keep trying to build a tool that logs every step of my automation script, but I feel like I'm reinventing the wheel compared to how I handle logging in Python. In Python I just use standard handlers and file redirection, so why does Rust seem to force me into this tracing_subscriber test layer setup when I only need simple assertions?

You are right that the Python approach feels simpler for basic logging, but in Rust you can't just redirect stdout because the compiler enforces strict ownership and thread safety rules. The tracing_subscriber crate provides a test layer specifically designed to capture events emitted by the tracing system without needing external files or complex configuration during your tests. This allows you to assert on individual log messages directly within your test function.

I've tried using serde_json::Value to parse the captured logs but I keep hitting errors because my tracing events don't match the expected schema. Can I just use a generic string capture instead of relying on specific event types?

Using raw strings is possible but loses type safety which defeats the purpose of Rust testing, so you should define your own custom serializer that implements the Serialize trait to match your specific data structure. The tracing_subscriber test layer expects events in a format compatible with your existing code, so if your tool uses struct types for logging, those need to be serialized consistently before being captured by the test harness.

This is getting frustrating because I thought my simple automation script would work out of the box without all this boilerplate. How do I actually configure the tracing_subscriber so it only captures what matters for my assertions and not everything?

The key is to use a FilterLayer within your tracing setup that targets specific module names or field values before they reach the test layer. By configuring the subscriber with filters on only the relevant modules in your tool, you reduce noise and make your tests faster while still maintaining full control over which events get recorded for verification.

I'm trying to write tests for a new service I'm contributing to an open-source project, but I keep getting confused about how to capture tracing output. Is there a way to just read the logs like normal print statements?

That's a common misconception when you first start with Rust testing tools. You cannot simply use standard println! macros inside your tests because they write directly to stdout, which makes assertions unreliable in automated environments. Instead, you should use the tracing_subscriber crate specifically its test layer which intercepts log events and stores them in memory for assertions.
Oh okay so I shouldn't just print to stdout? Can I replace my println! calls with tracing logs and then assert on those?

Exactly, but you need to be careful about the setup. You should initialize your tracing configuration before running tests using tracing_subscriber's test layer which creates a special subscription that collects events into an in-memory buffer rather than sending them anywhere.
Wait, I thought I just needed to add println! instead of tracing? That sounds too simple. Why is this so complicated?

I understand that frustration since it seems more complex than a single println!. The key difference is that you're not replacing println! with tracing_subscriber; instead, your code should already use the tracing crate and its events trait for logging. You just need to ensure your test harness uses the special test layer so those logs get captured automatically.
So I shouldn't be using println! at all in production code? Just stick with tracing_subscriber entirely?

Not necessarily for production, but you should definitely use tracing_subscriber for your test setup. The real trick is that tracing_subscriber provides a dedicated test layer that works seamlessly with the events trait and allows you to assert on captured logs without needing any external tools or file redirection.
I'm still confused about how exactly I should set this up in my tests. Should I create a new subscriber manually?

You don't need to manually configure everything for testing since tracing_subscriber provides a built-in test layer you can enable with just one line of code before your tests run. This gives you access to the captured events through its internal buffer without needing any complex subscription setup or manual configuration.
